prompt: The example of the moth's wing versus a still leaf mainly serves to

choices.text: list the steps a bat follows when building its nighttime roost
choices.rationale: No roost-building steps are given.

choices.text: explain why leaves make better food for bats than insects do
choices.rationale: Bats do not eat leaves; the leaf is a contrast object.

choices.text: prove that moths are the only prey that bats are able to catch
choices.rationale: The passage does not limit bat prey to moths.

choices.text: illustrate how finely a bat can distinguish objects by echo
choices.rationale: Correct. The contrast shows the precision of echo-based perception.

explanation: The moth-versus-leaf example demonstrates the precision of echolocation.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: The passage is organized mainly by

choices.text: arguing that bees deserve far less scientific study
choices.rationale: The passage does not argue against study.

choices.text: comparing the honeybee with the wasp point by point
choices.rationale: No wasp comparison appears.

choices.text: describing a behavior and then explaining what it communicates
choices.rationale: Correct. It first describes the dance, then decodes its meaning.

choices.text: listing, one by one, the separate parts of a flower
choices.rationale: Flower anatomy is not listed.

explanation: The structure moves from behavior to interpretation.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

prompt: The sentence beginning 'The method is not a cure-all' mainly serves to

choices.text: explain, step by step, how the mesh nets are manufactured
choices.rationale: Mesh manufacturing is not explained.

choices.text: summarize the long history of one particular village
choices.rationale: No village history is summarized.

choices.text: introduce an entirely different water-gathering technology
choices.rationale: No new technology is introduced.

choices.text: acknowledge the conditions and upkeep that limit the technique
choices.rationale: Correct. It lays out the conditions and upkeep that limit the method.

explanation: The sentence concedes the method's limits.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

prompt: The passage develops its main idea primarily by

choices.text: comparing two different species of kelp growing side by side
choices.rationale: No two kelp species are compared.

choices.text: listing the steps a diver takes to explore a kelp forest safely
choices.rationale: No diving steps are listed.

choices.text: tracing the life of a single sea otter from birth to old age
choices.rationale: No otter's life story is traced.

choices.text: describing the forest's richness, then showing how easily it can collapse
choices.rationale: Correct. It first portrays the thriving forest, then the collapse into an urchin desert.

explanation: The structure moves from the forest's richness to its fragility.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the passage?

choices.text: It traces the life cycle of a single coral colony from birth to death.
choices.rationale: It does not trace a colony's life cycle.

choices.text: It compares coral to a familiar record, explains the method, then notes a difficulty.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage moves from a tree-ring analogy to the band-reading method to a closing challenge.

choices.text: It argues that coral reefs are endangered and must be legally protected.
choices.rationale: The passage makes no protection argument.

choices.text: It lists the steps a diver should take to collect a coral sample safely.
choices.rationale: No collection steps are given.

explanation: The passage proceeds from analogy to method to remaining difficulty.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.

prompt: The passage is organized mainly by

choices.text: stating a problem, then listing several solutions plants have evolved
choices.rationale: Correct. It poses the dispersal problem, then lists wind, hook, and fruit solutions.

choices.text: arguing that wind dispersal should be studied above all others
choices.rationale: The passage does not argue to prioritize one method.

choices.text: giving instructions for planting seeds at the right depth
choices.rationale: No planting instructions are given.

choices.text: comparing two specific plant species side by side in detail
choices.rationale: No two species are compared in detail.

explanation: The structure is a problem followed by a list of evolved solutions.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: The author calls the change 'a mere matter of paper' mainly to

choices.text: explain the precise steps for printing an official ballot
choices.rationale: No printing steps are given.

choices.text: argue that the reform was, in the end, not worth the trouble
choices.rationale: The passage treats the reform as important, not pointless.

choices.text: set up a contrast with the large effect the change actually had
choices.rationale: Correct. The phrase downplays the change to highlight its surprisingly large effect.

choices.text: suggest that paper ballots were too costly to produce widely
choices.rationale: Cost of ballots is not discussed.

explanation: The 'mere paper' phrase contrasts a small change with a large effect.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.

prompt: The author compares a series of locks to a staircase mainly to

choices.text: help readers picture how boats move up or down in steps
choices.rationale: Correct. The staircase image clarifies the step-by-step rise and fall of boats.

choices.text: prove that climbing stairs is harder than operating a lock
choices.rationale: The passage does not compare difficulty of stairs and locks.

choices.text: argue that staircases should be built beside every river
choices.rationale: No argument about building staircases appears.

choices.text: list the materials needed to construct a single lock chamber
choices.rationale: The comparison is not a materials list.

explanation: The staircase analogy is an explanatory image for stepwise elevation change.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.
